function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Amazon has publicly committed more than $10 billion to Project Kuiper and industry analysts have estimated total program cost at $16.5–20 billion, though Amazon does not separately disclose Kuiper capex in its 10-K segment reporting. SpaceX's S-1 discloses Connectivity segment capex for FY2025 and Q1 2026. Using the disclosed SpaceX Connectivity capex figures and Amazon's stated $10 billion commitment floor, assess what the two figures suggest about each company's relative stage of constellation deployment. What analytical limitation arises from comparing a segment-level disclosure (SpaceX) against a program-level commitment estimate (Amazon), and what specific line item in Amazon's 10-K would an analyst consult to triangulate Kuiper investment?

steps.reasoning_content:
This is a complex analytical question that requires:

1. SpaceX S-1 data on Connectivity segment capex for FY2025 and Q1 2026
2. Understanding of Amazon's Project Kuiper commitment ($10B+ disclosed, $16.5-20B estimated)
3. Analytical reasoning about relative deployment stages
4. Understanding of segment-level vs program-level disclosure limitations
5. Knowledge of Amazon's 10-K reporting structure for Kuiper

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 to get the Connectivity segment capex figures, and also search for Amazon's 10-K disclosures on Kuiper.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: The edgar_search for Amazon with "Project Kuiper" capex commitment returned no results. Let me try a broader search.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve the Connectivity segment capital expenditure (capex) figures for FY2025 and Q1 2026. Look for segment-level capex or capital expenditure data related to the Connectivity segment, including Starlink. Also look for any discussion of capital expenditures by segment in the MD&A or notes to financial statements.

results.content:
Based on the provided financial documents, the capital expenditure (capex) figures for the **Connectivity segment** are as follows:

*   **For the full year 2025 (FY2025):** **$4,178 million**
*   **For the first quarter of 2026 (Q1 2026):** **$1,332 million**

These figures are reported in the capital expenditure tables within the financial statements and Preamble sections.

### Key Context from the MD&A Discussion:
The year-over-year increases in Connectivity segment capex are consistently explained by the same primary drivers:

*   **Driver:** Higher satellite and ground equipment costs as the company continues to increase its number of satellites and grow its satellite network.
*   **Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025:** Capex increased by **$518 million** (from $814 million to $1,332 million).
*   **FY2025 vs. FY2024:** Capex increased by **$680 million** (from $3,498 million to $4,178 million).
*   **FY2024 vs. FY2023:** Capex increased by **$1,043 million** (from $2,455 million to $3,498 million).

In summary, the significant capital allocated to the Connectivity segment reflects ongoing, aggressive investment in the Starlink satellite network infrastructure.

Based on the provided excerpts from SpaceX's financial disclosures, here is the requested information:

### **Connectivity Segment Capital Expenditures by Year**
The capital expenditures for the Connectivity segment are broken down as follows:

| Period | Capital Expenditures |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Three Months Ended March 31, 2026** | $1,332 million |
| **Three Months Ended March 31, 2025** | $814 million |
| **Year Ended December 31, 2025** | $4,178 million |
| **Year Ended December 31, 2024** | $3,498 million |
| **Year Ended December 31, 2023** | $2,455 million |

**Year-over-Year Growth (Annual):**
*   **2025 vs. 2024:** Increased by $680 million (19.4%).
*   **2024 vs. 2023:** Increased by $1,043 million (42.5%).

The primary driver for these increases, as stated in the MD&A, is **"higher satellite and ground equipment costs as we continue to increase our number of satellites and grow our satellite network."**

---

### **Discussion of Cumulative Starlink Investment & Infrastructure Costs**
While the excerpts do not provide a single cumulative total for the entire Starlink program, they detail significant investments and strategic context:

1.  **Scale of the Constellation:** As of March 31, 2026, SpaceX had approximately **9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites** in Low-Earth Orbit.
2.  **Strategic Asset Investment:** The company explicitly states that a **"significant amount of launch capacity"** is dedicated to deploying its own Starlink satellites. The associated launch and satellite costs are capitalized within the **Connectivity segment** and depreciated over time. This represents a massive, ongoing capital commitment to build and maintain the network.
3.  **Future Spectrum & Capacity Investment:** SpaceX entered into agreements to acquire 65 MHz of U.S. spectrum and global Mobile Satellite Service licenses from EchoStar for **$19.6 billion** in equity and cash (pending regulatory approval). This is a major future capital commitment to expand network capabilities.
4.  **Costs Driving Capex Increases:** The year-over-year increases in Connectivity capital expenditures are explicitly linked to:
    *   Manufacturing and launching more satellites.
    *   Scaling ground equipment to support the growing subscriber base.
    *   Developing next-generation satellite production and kits (highlighted in R&D spending).

**In summary,** the financial data shows consistent and accelerating annual capital investment in the Connectivity segment, driven directly by the expansion of the Starlink satellite constellation and supporting ground infrastructure. The strategic narrative emphasizes that these investments are core to SpaceX's vertical integration model and are essential for growing the global satellite internet service.

arguments.prompt: In {{amazon_10k_2025}}, retrieve all disclosures related to Project Kuiper, including any capital expenditure commitments, investment amounts, regulatory milestones (FCC), satellite deployment progress, and any segment-level capital expenditure data. Also look for any discussion of property and equipment additions that relate to Project Kuiper or satellite infrastructure.

In {{amazon_10k_2025}}, retrieve the segment reporting information, including capital expenditures by segment. Look for any mention of capex by operating segment, particularly for any segment that might include Project Kuiper (such as "North America", "International", "AWS", or "Other"). Also look for the discussion of property and equipment, including satellite-related assets.

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the Amazon 2025 Form 10-K, here is a synthesis of all disclosures specifically related to **Project Kuiper** and its associated satellite infrastructure. The information is integrated across multiple notes and sections.

### **1. Accounting Treatment & Expense Recognition**
*   **Current Expensing:** The company "currently expense[s] the majority of the costs associated with the development of [its] satellite network for global broadband service." This includes:
    *   Production costs
    *   Launch costs
    *   Payroll costs
    *   **Launch service deposits**, which are expensed upon the occurrence of the launch.
*   **Future Capitalization:** The company states it will "capitalize certain of these costs once the service achieves **commercial viability, including sales to customers**."
*   **Balance Sheet Classification (Pre-Capitalization):** Costs such as **satellite network launch services deposits** are classified within **"Other assets"** on the consolidated balance sheets. Upon achieving commercial viability, these will be reclassified to **"Construction in progress"** within **"Property and equipment, net."**

### **2. Capital Expenditure & Investment Context**
*   While **Project Kuiper is not broken out as a separate line item** in the capex tables, it is a key component of Amazon's overarching strategic investment.
*   The company explicitly links its massive infrastructure spending to supporting initiatives "including the development of a satellite network for global broadband service."
*   The significant year-over-year increases in **"Total net additions to property and equipment"**—particularly within the **AWS segment** ($96.5 billion in 2025)—reflect the scale of investment in technology infrastructure, which encompasses satellite-related assets.

### **3. Regulatory Milestone & Commitment**
*   A specific **Federal Communications Commission (FCC) milestone** is disclosed as a key regulatory requirement. The company must have **half of its satellite constellation (1,618 satellites) deployed and operational by July 30, 2026**.

### **4. Segment Reporting**
*   There is **no dedicated "Satellite" or "Kuiper" operating segment**. Project Kuiper costs and investments are housed within the **"Technology and infrastructure"** cost category, which is allocated across segments based on usage.
*   The primary segment for related capital assets would be **Amazon Web Services (AWS)**, given that satellite network infrastructure is listed alongside data centers as a key technology investment.

### **5. Financial Disclosure Summary**
| Disclosure Category | Key Details from 10-K |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Cost Recognition** | R&D, production, launch, and payroll costs are **expensed as incurred**. Launch deposits are expensed upon launch. |
| **Future Capitalization** | Costs will be capitalized upon achieving **commercial viability and first customer sales**. |
| **Balance Sheet Asset** | Launch service deposits are in **"Other assets."** Upon commercial viability, reclassified to **"Construction in progress"** (Property & Equipment). |
| **Regulatory Commitment** | FCC milestone: **50% of constellation (1,618 sats) deployed and operational by July 30, 2026**. |
| **Capital Expenditure Scale** | Integrated into the multi-billion dollar growth in **Technology and infrastructure** and **Property and equipment, net**, especially within the AWS segment. |
| **Segment Allocation** | Costs are allocated across segments under **"Technology and infrastructure."** Related assets reside in AWS segment reporting. |

### **Conclusion**
The disclosures indicate that as of the end of 2025, Project Kuiper remains in a heavy **investment and development phase**, with costs primarily impacting the income statement as R&D expenses. The company treats it as a strategic, cross-functional initiative rather than a standalone reporting segment. The major upcoming financial inflection point will be the transition to **capitalizing assets** upon the service's commercial launch, which will then increase property and equipment values and shift the cost profile toward depreciation. The most concrete external commitment is the **FCC deployment deadline in mid-2026**.

# Segment Reporting: Capital Expenditures and Property & Equipment

Based on the document excerpts you provided, here is the complete segment reporting information I was able to retrieve:

---

## 1. Capital Expenditures by Segment (Total Net Additions to Property & Equipment)

The document defines total net additions as including "technology infrastructure assets and the effect of non-cash activity such as property and equipment acquired but not yet paid."

| Segment | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **North America** (1) | $17,529M | $24,348M | $35,919M |
| **International** (1) | $4,144M | $6,643M | $7,617M |
| **AWS** (2) | $24,843M | $53,267M | $96,496M |
| **Corporate** | $1,828M | $1,494M | $2,320M |
| **Consolidated** | $48,344M | $85,752M | $142,352M |

**Key observation:** AWS capex nearly quadrupled from 2023 to 2025, growing from ~$24.8B to ~$96.5B, reflecting massive technology infrastructure investment. Overall consolidated capex tripled over the same period.

---

## 2. Property & Equipment, Net by Segment

| Segment | Dec 31, 2023 | Dec 31, 2024 | Dec 31, 2025 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **North America** | $93,632M | $103,041M | $122,043M |
| **International** | $24,357M | $25,618M | $30,632M |
| **AWS** | $72,701M | $110,683M | $190,055M |
| **Corporate** | $13,487M | $13,323M | $14,295M |
| **Consolidated** | $204,177M | $252,665M | $357,025M |

---

## 3. Segment Assets (Total, Reconciled)

| Segment | Dec 31, 2023 | Dec 31, 2024 | Dec 31, 2025 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **North America** | $196,029M | $210,120M | $235,652M |
| **International** | $69,718M | $69,487M | $81,984M |
| **AWS** | $108,533M | $155,953M | $252,588M |
| **Corporate** | $153,574M | $189,334M | $247,818M |

**Notes from the document:**
- North America and International segment assets primarily consist of **property and equipment, operating leases, inventory, accounts receivable, and digital video and music content**
- AWS segment assets primarily consist of **property and equipment, accounts receivable, and operating leases**

---

## 4. Depreciation & Amortization by Segment

| Segment | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **North America** | $13,678M | $14,285M | $15,503M |
| **International** | $4,016M | $4,462M | $4,907M |
| **AWS** | $12,531M | $13,320M | $21,450M |
| **Consolidated** | $30,225M | $32,067M | $41,860M |

**Notable:** AWS D&A jumped ~61% in 2025, partly driven by the change in useful life estimate for servers and networking equipment (from 6 to 5 years), which increased D&A by $1.4 billion in 2025 and primarily impacted the AWS segment.

---

## 5. Project Kuiper / Satellite-Related Assets

**The document does NOT break out satellite-related capital expenditures as a separate line item or by segment.** However, there are several relevant references:

### Direct mentions of the satellite network:

1. **In the Technology & Infrastructure costs definition:**
   > *"Collectively, these costs reflect the investments we make in order to offer a wide variety of products and services to our customers, including expenditures related to initiatives to build and deploy innovative and efficient software and electronic devices and **the development of a satellite network for global broadband service** and autonomous vehicles for ride-hailing services."*

2. **In the MD&A's investment strategy section:**
   > *"We seek to invest efficiently in several areas of technology and infrastructure, including AWS, and expansion of new and existing product categories and service offerings, as well as in infrastructure to enhance the customer experience and improve our process efficiencies."*

### How satellite assets are allocated:

The document explains that **technology infrastructure assets are allocated among segments based on usage**:

> *"Technology infrastructure assets, which are included in property and equipment, net, net additions, and the depreciation and amortization expense on these assets, are allocated among the segments based on usage, with the majority allocated to the AWS segment. Usage of technology infrastructure assets by the North America and International segments, and the related allocation of total net additions, can fluctuate on a quarter-to-quarter basis, and is affected by seasonality, peak periods, new product or service offerings, and other factors."*

### How infrastructure costs flow:

> *"Infrastructure costs include servers, networking equipment, and data center related depreciation and amortization, rent, utilities, and other expenses necessary to support AWS and other Amazon businesses."*

---

## 6. Key Structural Findings

### There are only three reportable segments — no "Other" segment:
> *"We have organized our operations into **three segments: North America, International, and AWS**."*

### Segment definitions:
- **North America:** Retail sales, advertising, and subscription services through North America-focused online and physical stores
- **International:** Retail sales, advertising, and subscription services through internationally-focused online stores
- **AWS:** Global sales of compute, storage, database, and other services

### Allocation methodology:
- Operating expenses (Fulfillment, Technology and infrastructure, Sales and marketing, General and administrative) are allocated to segments **based on usage**
- The majority of technology costs are incurred in the U.S. and included in North America and AWS segments
- The majority of infrastructure costs are allocated to the AWS segment

### Corporate assets (excluded from segments):
> *"Total segment assets exclude corporate assets, such as cash and cash equivalents, marketable securities, other long-term investments, corporate facilities, goodwill and other acquired intangible assets, and tax assets."*

---

## Summary

| Finding | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Reportable segments** | North America, International, AWS (3 total, no "Other") |
| **AWS capex growth** | $24.8B (2023) → $53.3B (2024) → $96.5B (2025) — nearly 4x growth |
| **Consolidated capex growth** | $48.3B (2023) → $142.4B (2025) — nearly 3x growth |
| **Satellite/Kuiper capex disclosure** | **Not separately broken out** by segment |
| **Satellite asset allocation** | Technology infrastructure allocated "based on usage, with the majority allocated to AWS segment" |
| **Satellite mentions** | Referenced in Technology & Infrastructure costs definition and investment strategy language |
| **Useful life change impact** | Server useful life reduced from 6→5 years, added $1.4B D&A in 2025, primarily impacting AWS |
| **Facility data (Dec 31, 2025)** | 741.5M sq ft leased + 78.1M sq ft owned; AWS had 28.4M sq ft leased + 28.5M sq ft owned |

The document explicitly notes that satellite development costs are part of the broader technology and infrastructure category, but does not isolate Project Kuiper capital expenditures as a separate disclosure within any segment. Based on the allocation methodology described, satellite-related infrastructure spending would flow through the segments based on usage, with the majority likely allocated to AWS, though some could flow to North America or other segments depending on the nature of the expenditure.

arguments.prompt: In {{amazon_10k_2025}}, retrieve the disclosure about "Other assets" on the balance sheet. Look for any mention of satellite network launch services deposits, or any line items that would contain Project Kuiper pre-commercial costs. Also retrieve any discussion of R&D expenses, technology and infrastructure costs, and any forward-looking statements about total investment expected for the satellite program. Look for any mention of the $10 billion commitment or FCC license obligations.

In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve information about cumulative total capital expenditures for the Connectivity segment or Starlink program. Also retrieve any discussion of the Starlink subscriber count and revenue growth trajectory.

results.content:
Based on the provided text from Amazon's financial filing, here is a synthesis of the information related to the "Other assets" balance sheet item, the accounting for satellite network launch deposits, and the broader context of technology/R&D costs and investment commitments for the satellite program.

### 1. **"Other assets" on the Balance Sheet**
The "Other assets" line item includes several components. Notably, it contains:
*   **Acquired identifiable intangible assets**, net of amortization.
*   **Satellite network launch services deposits**.
*   Other items like convertible notes, equity investments, long-term deferred tax assets, and affordable loans.

### 2. **Satellite Network Launch Services Deposits**
*   **Current Balance Sheet Location:** As of December 31, 2024 and 2025, these deposits are recorded within **"Prepaid expenses and other current assets,"** which is part of **"Accounts receivable, net and other."**
*   **Reclassification Criteria:** The company states it will **reclassify these deposits to "Construction-in-progress" within "Property and equipment, net"** once the satellite service **achieves commercial viability, including sales to customers.**
*   **Expense Recognition:** Until that point of capitalization, the company **currently expenses the majority of satellite network development costs** (including production, launch, payroll costs, and launch services deposits upon launch) to **"Technology and infrastructure."** This is confirmed in multiple sections.

### 3. **Technology and Infrastructure / R&D Costs**
*   **Definition:** "Technology and infrastructure costs" include:
    *   Payroll for R&D, store development, and system maintenance.
    *   Infrastructure costs for servers, networking, data centers (depreciation, rent, utilities, etc.), primarily supporting AWS.
*   **Satellite Program Inclusion:** This expense category explicitly includes **"expenditures related to initiatives to build and deploy... the development of a satellite network for global broadband service."**
*   **Accounting Policy:** The general policy is that these costs are **"expensed as incurred."** However, the specific exception for the satellite program is noted: costs will be **capitalized once the service achieves commercial viability.**
*   **Impact on Financials:** The increase in technology and infrastructure costs in 2025 was driven by infrastructure growth and depreciation.

### 4. **Forward-Looking Investment Commitments & Obligations**
*   **Overall Investment Strategy:** The company expects spending on technology and infrastructure (including AWS, AI/ML, and the satellite network) to **increase over time** to support long-term growth, which may **negatively impact short-term free cash flow.**
*   **Satellite Program Commitment:** The provided text **does not explicitly mention a "$10 billion commitment"** for the satellite program. However, the repeated emphasis on the program as a significant, capital-intensive "initiative" within a growth-focused investment strategy implies a major financial commitment.
*   **FCC License Obligation:** While the filing mentions **"licenses of digital media content"** as a reason for restricted cash, it **does not detail specific financial obligations tied to FCC licenses** for the satellite program in the provided excerpts.

### Summary
The accounting treatment for Project Kuiper's pre-commercial phase is clear: most costs are **expensed as "Technology and infrastructure."** The key deposits for launches are currently **classified as current assets ("Prepaid expenses")** and will move to the balance sheet as **property and equipment ("Construction-in-progress")** only after the service launches and begins generating revenue. The strategic rationale is part of a broader, long-term increase in technology investment to support growth, though specific details on a total program budget or FCC financial obligations are not present in the provided text.

Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1 prospectus, here is a synthesized summary of the key information regarding the Connectivity (Starlink) segment, focusing on cumulative investment, satellite deployment, growth metrics, and future capital plans.

### **1. Satellite Deployment & Network Scale**
*   **Satellites in Orbit:** As of March 31, 2026, SpaceX had **approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites** in Low-Earth Orbit (LEO), accounting for approximately 75% of all active maneuverable satellites in orbit.
*   **Mobile Constellation Growth:** The dedicated satellite-to-mobile "Starlink Mobile" constellation grew from approximately 360 V1 Mobile satellites in January 2025 to approximately 650 satellites as of March 31, 2026.
*   **Future Deployment (V3):** Deployment of next-generation **V3 satellites** (designed for 1 Tbps downlink capacity per satellite) is expected to begin in the second half of 2026 using Starship. A single Starship launch is expected to deploy up to 60 V3 satellites, representing a potential **20-fold increase in deployed downlink capacity** per launch compared to Falcon 9.

### **2. Subscriber Growth & Financial Performance**
*   **Subscriber Count:**
    *   **10.3 million** Starlink Subscribers as of March 31, 2026 (up ~105% from 5.0 million a year prior).
    *   Growth trajectory: 2.3M (2023) -> 4.4M (2024) -> 8.9M (2025) -> 10.3M (Q1 2026).
*   **Revenue Growth (Connectivity Segment):**
    *   **Q1 2026:** Revenue of $3,257 million (up 31.6% YoY).
    *   **Full Year 2025:** Revenue of $11,387 million (up 49.8% YoY).
    *   Growth drivers: Subscriber growth and increasing enterprise adoption (aviation, maritime, mobile).
*   **Average Revenue Per User (ARPU):** Declining due to international expansion and lower-priced plans.
    *   **Q1 2026:** $66/month (down 22.9% YoY).
    *   **Full Year 2025:** $81/month.
*   **Profitability:**
    *   **Segment Adjusted EBITDA (Q1 2026):** $2,087 million.
    *   **Segment Income from Operations (Full Year 2025):** $4,423 million (up 120.4% YoY).

### **3. Capital Investment & Future Needs**
*   **Historical Capital Expenditures (Connectivity Segment):**
    *   **Q1 2026:** $1,332 million.
    *   **Full Year 2025:** $4,178 million.
    *   **Full Year 2024:** $3,498 million.
    *   **Full Year 2023:** $2,455 million.
    *   *Note: These figures include satellite manufacturing, launches, and ground network infrastructure. A specific cumulative total for the Starlink program is not provided, but total Connectivity segment capex from 2023-2025 alone exceeded **$10 billion**.*
*   **Drivers of Capital Expenditure:** "Primarily driven by higher satellite and ground equipment costs as we continue to increase our number of satellites and grow our satellite network."
*   **Major Future Capital Commitment:**
    *   **EchoStar Spectrum Acquisition:** In 2025, SpaceX agreed to acquire 65 MHz of U.S. spectrum and global MSS licenses from EchoStar for **$19.6 billion** in equity and cash. Closing is expected in November 2027, subject to regulatory approvals.
    *   This spectrum is expected to "provide stronger support for current performance and potential future services," indicating a major investment in capacity and capability expansion.

### **4. Strategic Context & Forward-Looking Statements**
*   **Strategic Value:** The Space segment's launch capacity is strategically allocated to the Connectivity and AI segments. Launches for these internal purposes are capitalized within those segments and are not reflected as revenue in the Space segment.
*   **Market Opportunity:** Starlink's 10.3 million subscribers represent "a small fraction of the estimated 3.3 billion potential end users in the markets we currently serve."
*   **Growth Strategy:** Plans to grow subscribers through expanded retail distribution, marketing, and demonstrating superior service quality. Enterprise growth is targeted through direct sales in aviation, maritime, and land mobility.

### **Summary**
The excerpts indicate that SpaceX has made massive cumulative investments in the Starlink constellation, deploying thousands of satellites to achieve significant scale (10.3M subscribers, $11.4B annual revenue by 2025). While current capital expenditures run in the billions annually, a further step-change is planned with the deployment of higher-capacity V3 satellites on Starship and a **$19.6 billion commitment** to acquire strategic spectrum, signaling continued heavy investment to capitalize on a vast remaining market opportunity.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have comprehensive data from both filings. Let me compile my analysis.

Key findings:

**SpaceX Connectivity Segment Capex:**
- FY2023: $2,455M
- FY2024: $3,498M
- FY2025: $4,178M
- Q1 2026: $1,332M (annualized ~$5.3B)
- Cumulative 2023-2025: ~$10.1B
- Q1 2025: $814M

**Amazon Project Kuiper:**
- No separate segment capex disclosure
- $10B+ public commitment (from external knowledge, not in the 10-K itself)
- Industry estimates: $16.5-20B total program cost
- Currently expensing most costs as "Technology and infrastructure"
- Launch deposits in "Prepaid expenses and other current assets"
- Will capitalize upon commercial viability
- FCC milestone: 50% of constellation (1,618 sats) deployed by July 30, 2026
- Amazon's 10-K doesn't break out Kuiper capex by segment

**SpaceX:**
- ~9,600 satellites in orbit as of March 31, 2026
- 10.3 million subscribers
- Connectivity segment capex of ~$4.2B in FY2025
- Segment is profitable (Income from ops of $4.4B in 2025)
- Has cumulative capex >$10B over 2023-2025

**Analytical observations:**

1. **Relative stage of deployment:** SpaceX's Starlink is clearly in a mature deployment/operational phase with 9,600 satellites in orbit and 10.3M subscribers generating $11.4B in annual revenue with positive EBITDA. The capex figures of ~$4.2B annually reflect ongoing constellation maintenance and incremental growth rather than initial buildout. Amazon's $10B commitment floor (and estimated $16.5-20B total) represents a program still in pre-commercial deployment, as evidenced by the fact that Amazon hasn't yet reached "commercial viability" and is still expensing most Kuiper costs. The FCC deadline of July 2026 for 50% constellation deployment (1,618 satellites) suggests Amazon is still in the buildout phase.

2. **Analytical limitation:** Comparing SpaceX's segment-level capex (which includes all satellite manufacturing, launches, ground equipment for an already-operational constellation) to Amazon's program-level commitment estimate is problematic because:
   - SpaceX's capex reflects ongoing operational investment in a revenue-generating segment, while Amazon's commitment reflects total pre-commercial development costs
   - SpaceX's capex doesn't capture the full historical investment (earlier years not disclosed in the S-1)
   - The accounting treatments differ: SpaceX capitalizes satellite costs and depreciates them, while Amazon expenses most costs currently
   - SpaceX's segment capex includes costs that may be capitalized internally from the Space segment (launch services), making it not directly comparable to Amazon's external spending

3. **Amazon 10-K line item to consult:** To triangulate Kuiper investment, an analyst would consult:
   - **"Technology and infrastructure" expense line** on the income statement (where most Kuiper costs are expensed)
   - **"Prepaid expenses and other current assets" / "Other assets"** on the balance sheet (for satellite launch services deposits)
   - Look for changes in these line items year-over-year
   - The "Other assets" line specifically contains satellite network launch services deposits
   - An analyst could also look at the segment capex tables, particularly for AWS, though Kuiper costs are allocated across segments based on usage

arguments.final_result:
## SpaceX Connectivity Segment Capex vs. Amazon Project Kuiper Commitments: Comparative Analysis

### 1. Disclosed SpaceX Connectivity Segment Capex

| Period | Connectivity Capex |
|---|---|
| FY2023 | $2,455 million |
| FY2024 | $3,498 million |
| FY2025 | $4,178 million |
| Q1 2025 | $814 million |
| Q1 2026 | $1,332 million |
| **Cumulative FY2023–FY2025** | **$10,131 million** |

Source: SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026), Connectivity segment capital expenditure tables.

### 2. What These Figures Suggest About Relative Deployment Stages

**SpaceX Starlink — Mature Revenue-Generating Phase:**
- ~9,600 satellites in LEO as of March 31, 2026 (≈75% of all active maneuverable satellites in orbit)
- 10.3 million subscribers (doubled year-over-year from 5.0M)
- FY2025 Connectivity revenue of $11,387 million (+49.8% YoY)
- Segment income from operations of $4,423 million in FY2025; Adjusted EBITDA of $2,087 million in Q1 2026
- Annual capex of ~$4.2B reflects **ongoing incremental constellation replenishment, ground network expansion, and next-generation satellite development** — not initial buildout. The capex growth trajectory is decelerating (42.5% growth in FY2024 vs. FY2023, slowing to 19.4% in FY2025 vs. FY2024), consistent with a constellation that has already achieved operational scale.

**Amazon Project Kuiper — Pre-Commercial Deployment Phase:**
- Amazon's $10 billion public commitment represents the disclosed floor; industry estimates of $16.5–20 billion suggest the total program cost has not yet been fully incurred.
- Critically, Amazon's 10-K states it **"currently expense[s] the majority of the costs associated with the development of [its] satellite network"** and will only **"capitalize certain of these costs once the service achieves commercial viability, including sales to customers."** This confirms the service has not yet begun commercial customer sales.
- The FCC license requires **1,618 satellites (50% of constellation) deployed and operational by July 30, 2026** — a near-term deadline suggesting the constellation buildout is still underway.
- There is no subscriber count, revenue figure, or operating income disclosed for Kuiper, confirming it remains a cost center, not a revenue segment.

**Interpretation:** SpaceX's ~$4.2B in annual Connectivity capex reflects the capital intensity of **maintaining and expanding an already profitable, 9,600-satellite constellation serving 10+ million subscribers.** Amazon's $10B+ commitment floor reflects **cumulative pre-commercial development investment** in a constellation that has not yet achieved commercial viability. SpaceX has already transitioned to a reinvestment/growth phase; Amazon remains in the initial deployment and pre-revenue phase. The capital profiles are at fundamentally different points on the program lifecycle curve.

### 3. Analytical Limitation: Segment-Level Capex vs. Program-Level Commitment

Comparing SpaceX's segment-level capex disclosure to Amazon's program-level commitment estimate introduces several material analytical distortions:

**a) Mismatch in accounting treatment:**
- SpaceX **capitalizes** satellite manufacturing, launch costs, and ground equipment as property and equipment within the Connectivity segment, then depreciates them over useful life. The $4.2B FY2025 capex represents gross additions to the balance sheet.
- Amazon **expenses** most Kuiper costs (production, launch, payroll, launch deposits) as "Technology and infrastructure" on the income statement until commercial viability. The $10B+ commitment likely reflects a mixture of expensed and committed-but-not-yet-incurred costs, making it structurally incomparable to SpaceX's capitalized capex figure.

**b) Scope mismatch:**
- SpaceX's segment capex captures one year of investment in an operational constellation with an established asset base. It does not represent the total capital deployed to build Starlink from inception (pre-2023 figures are not disclosed in the S-1).
- Amazon's commitment figure represents cumulative or total-lifecycle program spending. A direct comparison of one year of SpaceX capex to Amazon's total commitment would dramatically understate SpaceX's cumulative investment.

**c) Internal transfer pricing:**
- SpaceX's Connectivity segment benefits from internally provided launch services from the Space segment, which are capitalized within Connectivity at cost rather than at market pricing. This means SpaceX's disclosed Connectivity capex includes launch costs at internal cost, while Amazon's program costs reflect external market-rate launch procurement (from ULA, Arianespace, and eventually Blue Origin). This understates the economic cost of SpaceX's launches relative to Amazon's.

**d) No Amazon segment equivalent:**
- Amazon does not have a satellite/Kuiper segment. Kuiper costs are embedded within "Technology and infrastructure" and allocated across North America, International, and AWS segments based on usage. There is no segment-level capex figure to compare directly.

### 4. Specific Amazon 10-K Line Items for Triangulating Kuiper Investment

Since Amazon does not separately disclose Kuiper capex, an analyst would consult the following line items to triangulate the investment:

**Primary line items:**

| Line Item | Location | What It Captures |
|---|---|---|
| **"Technology and infrastructure" expense** | Consolidated Statements of Operations | This is where the majority of Kuiper development costs (production, launch, payroll) are expensed. Year-over-year changes, combined with management's qualitative commentary, help bound the Kuiper contribution. |
| **"Prepaid expenses and other current assets"** | Consolidated Balance Sheets (within "Accounts receivable, net and other") | Contains **satellite network launch services deposits** — a direct, identifiable Kuiper-related asset that provides a lower bound on committed launch spending. |
| **"Other assets" (non-current)** | Consolidated Balance Sheets | Also contains satellite-related deposits and prepayments that have not yet been deployed. |

**Secondary/complementary items:**

| Item | Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Segment net additions to property and equipment (AWS segment)** | AWS capex grew from $53.3B (2024) to $96.5B (2025). While the vast majority reflects data center infrastructure, Kuiper-related capital assets that have been capitalized (post-commercial viability) would appear here. Before commercial viability, Kuiper costs bypass P&E entirely. |
| **Commitments and contingencies note** | May contain purchase obligations for satellite manufacturing or launch contracts. |
| **Restricted cash disclosures** | The 10-K notes restricted cash for certain obligations, which may include FCC-related commitments. |

**Analytical approach:** An analyst would estimate Kuiper's annual contribution by (1) examining the year-over-year growth in "Technology and infrastructure" costs and isolating the portion not attributable to AWS infrastructure growth (which dominates), (2) tracking changes in "Prepaid expenses" for launch deposit activity, and (3) reviewing management's qualitative commentary on investment priorities. The absence of a dedicated line item means any Kuiper capex estimate derived from Amazon's 10-K is inherently imprecise — a structural limitation that makes direct peer comparison with SpaceX's segment-level disclosure unreliable without significant adjustments for accounting treatment, scope, and program stage.

### Sources

| Source | URL |
|---|---|
| SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026) | https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm |
| Amazon 10-K (FY2025, filed Feb 6, 2026) | https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1018724/000101872426000004/amzn-20251231.htm |

### 4. Specific Amazon 10-K Line Items for Triangulating Kuiper Investment
